sections.heading: Symptoms
sections.content: Head and neck cancers encompass diverse subsites with distinct presentations. Oral cavity cancers (buccal mucosa, floor of mouth, oral tongue, alveolar ridge, retromolar trigone, hard palate) typically present as a non-healing ulcer or mass, with pain, dysphagia, or odynophagia [OR-1]. Oropharyngeal cancers (base of tongue, tonsil, soft palate, posterior pharyngeal wall) often present with a neck mass, especially in HPV-positive disease, along with sore throat, dysphagia, or otalgia [ORPH-1]. HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer is increasing in incidence, especially in men, and patients tend to be younger with less tobacco exposure [Discussion MS-3]. HPV-negative oropharyngeal cancer is linked to tobacco and alcohol, presenting with larger primaries and worse prognosis. Laryngeal cancers: glottic tumors cause early hoarseness, while supraglottic tumors present with dysphagia, otalgia, or neck mass; subglottic tumors are rare and present with stridor or dyspnea [GLOT-1, SUPRA-1]. Hypopharyngeal cancers (pyriform sinus, postcricoid, posterior pharyngeal wall) often present late with dysphagia, odynophagia, referred otalgia, and neck mass; prognosis is poor [HYPO-1]. Nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC) is endemic in Southeast Asia and associated with EBV; classic presentation includes cervical lymphadenopathy, epistaxis, nasal obstruction, conductive hearing loss, and cranial neuropathies [NASO-1]. Sinonasal tumors (ethmoid and maxillary sinus) present with unilateral nasal obstruction, epistaxis, facial pain, or proptosis; histologies include squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, esthesioneuroblastoma, and undifferentiated carcinoma [ETHM-1, MAXI-1]. Salivary gland tumors (parotid, submandibular, minor) typically present as a painless, slow-growing mass; malignancy is suspected with facial nerve palsy, rapid growth, or fixation [SALI-1]. Occult primary presents as a cervical lymph node metastasis without an identifiable primary after thorough workup [OCC-1]. Mucosal melanoma of the head and neck presents as a pigmented or non-pigmented lesion in the nasal cavity, sinuses, or oral cavity, often with epistaxis or mass [MM-1].

symptoms.name: Neck mass
symptoms.detail: Painless or painful cervical lymphadenopathy, often firm or fixed, may be cystic in HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer.
symptoms.frequency: Very common in oropharyngeal, hypopharyngeal, and nasopharyngeal cancers; may be first sign in occult primary.

symptoms.name: Hoarseness
symptoms.detail: Change in voice quality lasting >3 weeks; common in glottic laryngeal cancer.
symptoms.frequency: Present in >90% of glottic cancers, often early stage.

symptoms.name: Dysphagia/Odynophagia
symptoms.detail: Pain or difficulty swallowing, often with referred otalgia.
symptoms.frequency: Common in oropharyngeal, hypopharyngeal, and supraglottic laryngeal cancers.

symptoms.name: Oral ulcer or mass
symptoms.detail: Non-healing ulcer, erythroplakia, or leukoplakia in the oral cavity.

symptoms.name: Epistaxis and nasal obstruction
symptoms.detail: Unilateral epistaxis, nasal congestion, or discharge.
symptoms.frequency: Common in sinonasal and nasopharyngeal cancers.

symptoms.name: Otalgia
symptoms.detail: Referred ear pain, often unilateral, without otoscopic findings.
symptoms.frequency: Common in oropharyngeal, hypopharyngeal, and laryngeal cancers.

symptoms.name: Cranial neuropathy
symptoms.detail: Facial numbness, diplopia, or other cranial nerve deficits.
symptoms.frequency: More common in nasopharyngeal and sinonasal cancers due to skull base invasion.

symptoms.name: Visible or palpable primary lesion
symptoms.detail: Ulcerated, exophytic, or infiltrative mass in oral cavity, oropharynx, or larynx on examination.
symptoms.frequency: Present in most oral cavity and laryngeal cancers; oropharyngeal tumors may be occult.

symptoms.name: Cervical lymphadenopathy
symptoms.detail: Enlarged, firm, or matted lymph nodes in the neck; may be cystic in HPV-positive disease.
symptoms.frequency: Present in 30–60% of patients at diagnosis depending on subsite.

symptoms.name: Vocal cord immobility
symptoms.detail: Impaired or fixed vocal cord on laryngoscopy, indicating paraglottic space invasion.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Persistent neck mass >2 weeks requires urgent investigation, especially if >3 cm, fixed, or matted.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Persistent hoarseness >3 weeks warrants laryngeal examination.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Progressive dysphagia or odynophagia, especially with weight loss.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Lesion >2 weeks without healing, especially in high-risk patients (tobacco/alcohol).

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Unilateral symptoms, especially with associated cranial neuropathy or proptosis.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Persistent unilateral otalgia, especially with dysphagia or neck mass.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Any cranial nerve deficit, particularly V1–V3 or VI, warrants urgent imaging.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Multiple, bilateral, or fixed nodes suggest advanced disease.
